Ultimate Orienteer

The 2004 Ultimate Orienteer Series began on March 6 at Priest Pt. Park in Olympia. Each event in the series includes a different format of the sport we all love: Short-O, Score-O, Classic-O, and Long-O. The 2004 series follows:

  • March 6 Short-O, Priest Pt. Park, Olympia
  • April 17 Score-O, Fire Mt., Mt. Vernon
  • May 8 or 9 Classic-O, Cle Elum Ridge
  • June 19 Long-O, Teanaway Forks, Cle Elum

Scoring: results of the longest course at each event are used to calculate series points. The winner of the longest course at each event receives 1000 points and everyone else on that course receives points based on the percent behind the winner's time. For example, if the winner has a time of 25 minutes and your time is 50 minutes, you will score 500 points. Your highest 3 scores are combined to give your Ultimate Orienteer total.

Pre-registration in the series is NOT required. Simply running the longest course at a series event automatically enters you in the competition.

Frequent O' Card

You can purchase a Frequent O' Card, a pre-paid punchcard accepted at any Cascade event. As a bonus, after you use all the punches, you can use the card for a free $5 entry. Cards are $50 each and have 10 punch boxes worth $5 each. To get a punch card, send a check for $50 and a self-addressed, stamped envelope to:

Cascade Orienteering Club
P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, WA 98103

You can also purchase the cards at events from Debbie Newell. Please contact her before an event to ensure that she will not only be at that event, but will have a card for you.

Dues Increase

At the club's annual meeting, the board of directors decided to increase membership dues. This was necessary because of a large increase in insurance fees which we must pay to USOF's insurance carrier. Annual individual membership fees are now $14 and family membership fees are now $20.

Volunteer Appreciation Coupons

To thank meet volunteers and encourage more people to help, the board has decided to issue appreciation coupons to everyone who puts in at least 1.5 hours at an event. The coupon may be used to pay for an individual entry at a class C Cascade event or as redemption towards a Frequent O' Card.

Election of Officers

Don Atkinson, Don Denbo, Peter Golde, Eugen Groff, and Katie Sprugel were elected as the 2004 COC board of directors. The elected board members selected the following officers: President, Jennifer Knowles; Vice President, Eugen Groff; Secretary, Ardis Dull; and Treasurer, Katie Sprugel.

Mac MacDonald

[Black and white photo of a man in an orienteering suit holding a compass and map, standing in a sunny clearing.]
Mac at the 1991 Western States Champs in California. This photo appeared on the cover of O' magazine Orienteering North America.

Local orienteer Bill “Mac” MacDonald died February 19 of complications following recent elective heart surgery. Mac, a cardiologist, was an enthusiastic orienteer, setting courses and volunteering at local events as well as participating in national and international meets. Mac’s wife, Karen, often accompanied him to orienteering events and son Bill is an alumnus of the WIOL. Always friendly and interesting, Mac will be greatly missed.

Mac's first event was on December 6, 1986 at Kelsey Creek Park in Bellevue. He and his son, Bill, did both Course 1 (which Mac won) and Course 2 that day. That was the beginning of a long fascination with orienteering that eventually led Mac to participate in numerous events across the U.S. and Canada. His love of orienteering continued until the day he died. You might say Mac is still orienteering, as he was buried in his O' suit, his compass at the ready.

block_index.text: The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | First Quarter 2004 | www.CascadeOC.org | Page 1

The 2004 Ultimate Orienteer Series began on March 6 at Priest Pt. Park in Olympia. Each event in the series includes a different format of the sport we all love: Short-O, Score-O, Classic-O, and Long-O. The 2004 series follows:
March 6 Short-O, Priest Pt. Park, Olympia
April 17 Score-O, Fire Mt., Mt. Vernon
May 8 or 9 Classic-O, Cle Elum Ridge
June 19 Long-O, Teanaway Forks, Cle Elum

Scoring: results of the longest course at each event are used to calculate series points. The winner of the longest course at each event receives 1000 points and everyone else on that course receives points based on the percent behind the winner's time. For example, if the winner has a time of 25 minutes and your time is 50 minutes, you will score 500 points. Your highest 3 scores are combined to give your Ultimate Orienteer total.
Pre-registration in the series is NOT required. Simply running the longest course at a series event automatically enters you in the competition.

You can purchase a Frequent O' Card, a pre-paid punchcard accepted at any Cascade event. As a bonus, after you use all the punches, you can use the card for a free $5 entry. Cards are $50 each and have 10 punch boxes worth $5 each. To get a punch card, send a check for $50 and a self-addressed, stamped envelope to:
Cascade Orienteering Club P.O. Box 31375 Seattle, WA 98103
You can also purchase the cards at events from Debbie Newell. Please contact her before an event to ensure that she will not only be at that event, but will have a card for you.

At the club's annual meeting, the board of directors decided to increase membership dues. This was necessary because of a large increase in insurance fees which we must pay to USOF's insurance carrier. Annual individual membership fees are now $14 and family membership fees are now $20.

To thank meet volunteers and encourage more people to help, the board has decided to issue appreciation coupons to everyone who puts in at least 1.5 hours at an event. The coupon may be used to pay for an individual entry at a class C Cascade event or as redemption towards a Frequent O' Card.

Don Atkinson, Don Denbo, Peter Golde, Eugen Groff, and Katie Sprugel were elected as the 2004 COC board of directors. The elected board members selected the following officers: President, Jennifer Knowles; Vice President, Eugen Groff; Secretary, Ardis Dull; and Treasurer, Katie Sprugel.

[Black and white photo of a man in an orienteering suit holding a compass and map, standing in a sunny clearing.] Mac at the 1991 Western States Champs in California. This photo appeared on the cover of O' magazine Orienteering North America.

Local orienteer Bill “Mac” MacDonald died February 19 of complications following recent elective heart surgery. Mac, a cardiologist, was an enthusiastic orienteer, setting courses and volunteering at local events as well as participating in national and international meets. Mac’s wife, Karen, often accompanied him to orienteering events and son Bill is an alumnus of the WIOL. Always friendly and interesting, Mac will be greatly missed.

Mac's first event was on December 6, 1986 at Kelsey Creek Park in Bellevue. He and his son, Bill, did both Course 1 (which Mac won) and Course 2 that day. That was the beginning of a long fascination with orienteering that eventually led Mac to participate in numerous events across the U.S. and Canada. His love of orienteering continued until the day he died. You might say Mac is still orienteering, as he was buried in his O' suit, his compass at the ready.
